title: Sustainable Agriculture and Planetary Boundaries: Exploring the Socioeconomic and Socio-ecological footprints of Mexico’s Agricultural Sector.
text:
Description
Conventional agriculture, with its heavy reliance on agrochemicals, technology and extensive practices, poses significant threats to biodiversity, human health, and largely contributes to climate change, biodiversity loss and land use change. Yet, its role in providing widespread and affordable food is undeniable, creating a critical dichotomy with alternative, agroecological systems. The transition to sustainable farming is fraught with challenges, including high labour demands and (bio)inputs, and structural barriers, highlighting the urgent need for new approaches. Particularly, since both systems underpin differentiated socio-economic and socioecological profiles in their production practices.
To understand the real diversity and complexity of these agricultural practices, we processed a dataset covering more than 50,000 Mexican farms. We assembled farming archetypes based on observed practices, to which we coupled socioeconomic and socio-ecological footprints.
OBS! This work took place while attending the Young Scientists Summer Program at the International Institute for Applied Systems Analysis (IIASA). A Preliminary harmonised dataset is available.

Historical Agronomic Dataset - Insights from Mexico (2012 - 2022). Expanded and harmonized version. (early view, SQL)

title: The Hidden Cost of Biodiversity and Land Use: How Ecological Models Shape our Biodiversity Metrics.
text:
Description
Land-use and land-use change (LULUC) remain the primary drivers of terrestrial biodiversity loss globally. Quantifying these impacts has a long-standing tradition within frameworks like Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) and Environmentally Extended Multi-Regional Input-Output (EE-MRIO) models. Yet, many of these frameworks have different entry points to assess the cause-effect chains that LULUC have on species and ecosystems.
In this piece, we assess and compare 11 biodiversity metrics at the ecoregion level at the global scale for five species groups (amphibians, birds, mammals, reptiles and vascular plants). With this endevour we aim to uncover where models (dis)agree and why.
OBS! We are actively working on this piece and the submission should take place during the summer. Stay tunned for the pre-print and the associated database.

title: Daniel IAO
text:
Welcome to my research, consultancy and opinion portfolio. My work focuses on sustainability sciences at large with a special focus on industrial ecology, social-ecological systems and social ecology. While I mostly engage with quantitative analyses, I have also explored and applied qualitative methodologies spanning workshops, reviews, interviews, among others. At my core, I approach this with a complex systems lens.
This portal serves as an interactive hub for my on-going projects, including materials prepared for the World Biodiversity Forum at Davos, Switzerland.
My ongoing projects are mostly associated with my PhD at the Stockholm Resilience Centre | Stockholm University, including:



Accounting for biodiversity impacts of consumption and production: current gaps and frontiers.
The Hidden Cost of Biodiversity and Land Use: How Ecological Models Shape our Biodiversity Metrics.
Sustainable Agriculture and Planetary Boundaries: Exploring the Socioeconomic and Socio-ecological footprints of Mexico’s Agricultural Sector.

title: Accounting for biodiversity impacts of consumption and production: current gaps and frontiers.
text:
Description
Current production and consumption patterns are the primary drivers of global biodiversity loss. While companies and governments increasingly attempt to calculate their “biodiversity footprints”, the metrics they rely on are vast, wide and diverse.
To understand the landscape of biodiversity footprinting, we conducted a systematic map using NLP models (not AI!) to filter over 1.2 million scientific records aiming at identifying “what is measured and how” when we talk about impacts on biodiversity.
OBS! We are actively working on this piece after a peer-review round. Stay tunned for the pre-print and the associated database.


Download Pre-print (PDF; in peer-review)
